text: Continuing Achilles tendon-loading activity (running and jumping) using a pain-monitoring model during rehabilitation produces no worse outcomes than complete rest from such activities for the first 6 weeks.

A graduated Achilles tendon-loading strengthening program produces significant improvements in VISA-A-S scores from baseline (~57) to 12 months (~85-91) regardless of whether running is continued or paused.
A pain-monitoring model can be used to safely guide continued running and jumping during Achilles tendinopathy rehabilitation without negative effects on recovery.
Complete rest from Achilles tendon-loading activities is not necessary for successful Achilles tendinopathy rehabilitation when a structured loading program and pain monitoring are in place.

Rehabilitation protocols that gradually increase load on the Achilles tendon and calf muscle produce significant improvements in both pain and function for Achilles tendinopathy patients over 12 months.
specific_value: P < .01 at all evaluation time points

text: The group that continued running during Achilles tendinopathy rehab achieved a VISA-A-S score of 85 at 12 months, while the rest group achieved 91, with no statistically significant difference between groups.
